Introduction
Document scope
1.1 Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case and assign it to the relevant queue.
1.2 Intended users
Table 1.1 Definition of persons who can use or work on the equipment
Term Definition
Authorized A person that is permitted by the user to use or do work on the equipment by
person the User of the equipment.
Note: The authorized person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
A person that is approved by Elekta to have the necessary knowledge and
Elekta approved training to do specified tasks on Elekta equipment.
person Note: The authorized person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Operator A person who operates the equipment with or without help from an assistant,
and who controls some or all the functions of the equipment.
Note: The operator must have the correct level of knowledge and training
before using or doing work on the equipment.
Clinical User A person who operates the equipment for the treatment of patients.
Note: A Clinical User must be a qualified person who has the necessary
knowledge and training in the safe, clinical operation of the equipment. Such
treatment must be therapeutic only.
Clinical User – The role of Radiation Therapists, also referred to as Radiotherapists, Radiation
Radiation Treatment Technicians or Therapeutic/Therapy radiographers, may be
Therapist different based on their geographical region and experience/qualifications.
However, their primary roles and obligations are expected to be similar among
different regions and sites.
Note: Radiation Therapists are trained on all aspects of system operation, but
not trained on installation, calibration or service maintenance.
Note: Radiation Therapists who work at the site when the system is installed
will be trained by an Elekta trainer. After the system is introduced, Elekta
authorized trainers, or experienced Radiation Therapists who are already
trained on the equipment, can train Radiation Therapists who are new to the
equipment.

Introduction
Intended users
Term Definition
Clinical User – Dosimetrists design, generate, and measure radiation dose distributions and
Medical dose calculations in collaboration with the Medical Physics Expert and
Dosimetrist Radiation Oncologist.
Note: Dosimetrists are trained on the general principles of system operation,
but not trained on installation, calibration, or service maintenance.
Medical Physics A person that is recognized by a competent authority to have the necessary
Expert (MPE) knowledge and training in radiation physics, or radiation treatment, to give
Radiation information on patient dosimetry, use of radiation procedures and equipment,
Protection on optimization, quality assurance, do the applicable tests and calculations for
Advisor (RPA) radiation levels and dose assessments, and other items related to medical
radiation exposure and safety.
Note: Medical Physicists are trained on performing system QA procedures and
calibration or service maintenance tasks.
Radiation A physician whose trained medical specialty is the use of radiation therapy as
Oncologist a treatment for cancer.
Radiotherapy A Nurse with specific focus on the Radiation Oncology clinic patient support.
Nurse Certain countries utilize specially trained nurses to run the Linear Accelerator.
Qualified person A person that is recognized by a competent authority to have the necessary
knowledge and training to do specified tasks.
Note: The qualified person must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Qualified Expert A person that is recognized by a competent authority to do the applicable
(QE) tests and calculations for radiation levels and dose assessments. The person
also provides information on the necessary correct radiation protection and
procedures.
Note: The qualified expert must have the correct level of knowledge and
training before using or doing work on the equipment.
Service User - A Service User operates the equipment to do maintenance tasks. A Service
Service engineer User is a qualified person who has the necessary knowledge and training to
set different software configurations, do the necessary tests, adjustments,
optimization, and calibration procedures on the equipment. Such operation
must not be therapeutic.
Note: The Service User must have the correct level of knowledge and training
before using or doing work on the equipment.
User The organization or person responsible for the operation and maintenance of
the equipment.
Note: The user must have the correct level of knowledge and training before
using or doing work on the equipment.
For more information on persons that can work on the equipment, and on training, contact your
local Elekta representative.

1.4 Glossary of terms
Table 1.3 Glossary of terms
Term Definition
Autotrack function The autotrack function automatically moves the diaphragms or
leaves to their prescribed position.
Cal block The Cal block contains the item and part values that, when
loaded, configure the linear accelerator for a given energy.
Clinical Mode In Clinical Mode, Clinical Users use the linear accelerator and its
accessories for the radiation therapy treatment that a licensed
medical practitioner prescribes.
Gating Gating is the procedure by which an external system or manual
task pauses and starts the radiation delivery as a result of
information received relating to the treatment position of the
patient.
Interdigitation Interdigitation is an overlap of the opposite adjacent leaves of a
multileaf collimator.
Service life Service life is the length of time that the regular servicing and
repair procedures can keep the machine in operation.
Service Mode In Service Mode, Service Users do the necessary maintenance
tasks and calibration of the equipment.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
2.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, maintain, and dispose of the equipment according to the
information in the product documentation, and in compliance with local regulations. For the
safety of the users and the patients, before a user operates or works on the equipment, they
must read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, instructions, and notes in this document and the related
documents.
• Safety instructions, emergency procedures, and information in this document and the
related documents.
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment.
If you have local work instructions for the equipment, then Elekta recommends that you include
the warnings, cautions, and notes from this document in your local work instructions.
2.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 2.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 2.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
2.1.2 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can
keep the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING 2.2
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year. If the equipment is used more frequently than this, the frequency of the
planned maintenance must increase accordingly and the extended life checks must also be
brought forward accordingly.

General safety information
Important safety instructions
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the applicable Instructions for Use.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

2.2 Safety precautions
2.2.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 2.3
Do not use parts, accessories, or other equipment, without approval by Elekta of their
compatibility. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance
of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the
immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
2.2.2 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.

Document ID: 45133702098_09 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator

Page 18 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 2.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 2.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
2.2.3 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 2.6
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 2.7
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct

General safety information
Safety precautions
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
2.2.4 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 2.8
Do not connect any electrical power supply before the ground earth connections are secure.
There is a risk of high leakage current. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.9
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the covers. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
2.2.5 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 2.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
2.2.6 Safety precaution for work on BLDs with UV lighting
WARNING 2.11
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the BLD without protective glasses. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
The covers and screens of the BLD prevent an injury to your eyes from the ultraviolet lighting.
Before you remove the covers or screens of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator, or use
protective glasses. Protective glasses must have the UV-A specification OD 6+ @ 190 - 532 Nm.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator and contact
the Elekta representative.

Document ID: 45133702098_09 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.7 Safety precaution for work with lasers
WARNING 2.12
Do not look into the laser beams because they can cause damage to your eyes. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Elekta systems use the laser beams to help set up patients in the correct position for treatment.
During your work in the treatment room, make sure that you do not look into the laser beams.
2.2.8 Safety precautions for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 2.13
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
2.2.9 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 2.14
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.15
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 2.2

2.2.10 Safety precautions for work with high temperatures on the BLD
covers
During normal operation, the maximum temperatures of the accessory ring and BLD outer covers
can increase to 45 °C. Make sure that you do not touch the outer covers or the accessory ring for
more than 1 minute.
2.2.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 2.16
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
2.2.12 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
The safety precautions for work with heavy parts are the necessary safety precautions to know to
prevent injury.
WARNING 2.17
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
2.2.13 Safety precautions during work on the BLD
When you do work on the BLD, make sure that you obey the following safety precautions:
WARNING 2.18
Do not do work on the inner parts of the beam limiting device while the electrical supply is
connected. There is a risk of high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.19
Do not do work on the beam limiting device unless the gantry is at 0° or 180°. Gravity can cause
the leaves, diaphragms, and dynamic leaf guides to move. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 2.20
Do not touch the hot LED field light projector, or the heat sink, immediately after operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.14 Safety precaution for the removal of parts from the radiation head
WARNING 2.21
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
After the operation of the linear accelerator at high energy, the parts in the radiation head can
become radioactive and a radiation hazard. Before you do work on, or remove parts from the
radiation head, use a calibrated radiation detector to measure the level of induced radioactivity.
Use the applicable safety procedures when you remove radioactive parts and material. The
maximum dose rate for the safe removal of radioactive parts and material is 50 µSv/hr
(microsievert per hour). Do not remove the radioactive parts and material from the treatment
room before they are correctly prepared for safe removal.
2.2.15 Safety precautions for maintenance, modification, and
decommissioning
WARNING 2.22
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only do work on, or make modifications to, the equipment if you are an Elekta approved person
with the applicable authority. Such work must use best practice and obey the laws and
regulations in the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Modifications or work that does not have approval by Elekta, and is not done by an Elekta
approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.
2.2.16 Safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity are the precautions to prevent
an excessive exposure to, and serious injury from, induced radioactivity.
When you operate or do work on the equipment, Elekta recommends that you use the applicable
radiation protection functions, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity for the jurisdiction in which the
equipment is installed.
The safety precautions are applicable to:
• Maintenance, modifications, and decommissioning
• Measurement of the induced radioactivity
• Removal and movement of parts with induced radioactivity.

General safety information
Safety precautions
2.2.16.1 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious
injury or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the
last calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period
of time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING 2.23
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
2.2.16.2 Safety precautions for handling parts with induced radioactivity
The handling of parts with induced radioactivity must comply with the laws and regulations of the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK
do not apply in all jurisdictions. The permitted level of radioactivity must always be as low as
reasonably achievable (ALARA). This level may vary depending on local rules, therefore the safe
working levels from the applicable local rules must be considered as the acceptable working limit.
If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, use the UK value as a guideline, which is a
maximum of 150 cps at the surface for a maximum duration of 72 minutes. The following table
shows the conversion from this value to other units.

The regulations for training give a description of the necessary training, regulations, and

Different countries have different regulations for training. Make sure that you have the necessary

3.1.3 Disposal of the equipment
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, may remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material.

Information. This document is available on request from Elekta.

HDDs, remove them from the device and hand them to the customer for disposal. If the customer
entrusts the disposal team to dispose of the data, the data must be destroyed in accordance with
relevant cybersecurity regulations.

Description of the beam transport system
4.1 Description of the beam transport system
The beam transport system sends the electron beam through the waveguide to the collimator.
The waveguide increases the speed of the beam.
1 2 4 5
112°
44°
44°
112°
3
Elevation showing
steering coil positions
014065_001
Figure 4.1 Beam transport control system
(1) Gun cathode (2) Focus coils
(3) Steering coils (4) Center joint
(5) Bending magnets
The source of electrons is the electron gun in the RF system. The electron gun discharges
electrons from its filament. The electrostatic effect of the gun focus electrode, makes the
electrons focus into a beam. The electrons then go through a hole in the anode and into the
waveguide which increases the speed of the electrons. In the waveguide, a number of focus coils
give a static axial magnetic field which has an effect on the electron beam. The focus coils are
around the waveguide along the first half of its length.
During travel, a number of centering coils give specified movements to the electron beam to stay
in the center of the waveguide:
• A radial movement (R) in the plane of bending.
• A transverse movement (T) perpendicular to the plane of bending.
Two pairs of centering coils - 1R and 1T at the input end of the waveguide, put the beam in the
center. These coils are set at right angles to each other and to the beam, this gives two,
orthogonal, transverse magnetic fields.
As the electron beam goes through the waveguide, external forces can have an effect on the
beam. These external forces can cause small movements of the beam which change with the
gantry angle. To adjust for movements, a second pair of centering coils (2R and 2T) is installed.
They are installed near to the output end of the waveguide and out of the effects of the focus
field.
The centering coils give two orthogonal, transverse magnetic fields. These are servo-controlled to
keep the electron beam at the correct point of entry into the field of the beam bending system.
Look-up tables (LUT) set the servo target point to a center position in the current window at
different gantry angles.
Not controlled when downloaded or printed

© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 37

Product description
Description of the beam transport system
From the waveguide, the electron beam goes through a bellows section. The bellows let the full
flight tube assembly move in the beam bending system. This mechanism is known as the target
shift. The target shift system lets an X-ray target or an electron window be accurately put in
position. The position is at the top of the BLD to agree with the selected mode of operation.
From the bellows, the electron beam goes through the beam bending system which has an
achromatic triple magnet array in an evacuated flight tube. The magnet array (slalom) gives a
double focus of the electron beam. This moves the beam through 112° (from 22° above the
horizontal to 90° below).
In the X-ray mode, the beam hits the center of the target at right angles to cause a symmetrical X-
ray output. The effect of flattening filters in the collimator changes the beam to a flat X-ray field in
the treatment plane.
In the electron mode, the beam goes through a thin metal ‘window’ in the center into the
collimator. In the collimator, the beam is scattered by a dual-foil configuration to give a constant
beam.
4.1.1 Description of the servo-control of the beam
The path that the beam follows in the beam bending system is controlled by the conditions that
follow:
• The field strength of each of the magnets
• The beam energy
• The position of entry into the magnetic field.
Very close control of these parameters is necessary to make sure that there are stable, flat field
conditions. The currents in the coils of the slalom bending magnets are controlled and made
stable at levels given by the selected energy. See Figure 4.2

Product description
Description of the beam shaping system
(1) Gun (2) Magnetic shield
(3) Linear accelerator (4) Achromatic triple magnet
(5) Flight tube inside magnet (6) Target or window selection
(7) Radiation head (8) Ion chamber with beam position and
energy monitoring plates
A compound ion chamber assembly is installed adjacent to the secondary filter carrier. The ion
chamber monitors the radiation dose, and gives servo control of the electron beam in the
accelerating wave guide. The ion chamber has two, isolated, dose monitoring chambers. There is
also a chamber with plates that monitors changes in the flatness and symmetry of the applicable
beam.
In the X-ray mode, with the RF power in the waveguide constant, very high beam current (large
number of electrons) is needed to produce a clinically usable dose rate. This causes significant
beam loading such that a change in the beam current causes a change in the energy of the
electron beam. Therefore, in X-ray mode, a servo controls the beam energy by controlling the gun
emission. This keeps the beam energy constant by using the beam energy data from the ion
chamber.
In the electron mode, the beam current can be very much lower to achieve the required dose rate
so the beam loading is negligible. Because of this the gun emission controls the dose rate, not the
beam energy. Therefore the servo controls the gun emission to keep a constant dose rate.
The 2R and 2T centering coils control the position of the entry of the beam into the bending field.
This gives the flat field conditions.
The ion chamber supplies the control signals for the 2R and 2T coils. In the plane of bending, two
pairs of monitoring segments (inner and outer) are available. In this condition the outer pair are
not used. The inner pair of segments is sensitive to centering errors, and therefore uses the servo
system that controls the current in the 2R centering coils.
In the transverse direction to the plane of bending, a pair of segments monitors the two edges of
the field from which an error signal is derived.
The difference between the two signals from these plates is fed back to the control processor of
the treatment control system (TCS). This is used to control the current in the 2T centering coils at
the output end of the linear accelerator.
This gives servo-control of transverse symmetry in the X-ray and electron beam modes. This keeps
the transverse symmetry accurate and only the gantry angle LUT controls the current in the 2T
centering coils.

4.2.1 Description of the primary (scatter) filter system
The primary filter assembly has a movable carrier that operates between the target/electron
window and the top of the collimator. Five of the filter positions contain different thicknesses of
tantalum which is an electron scattering material. The level of the electron energy gives the
thickness of the tantalum. The sixth position has an open hole used in the X-ray mode.
The software selects the applicable filter and energy this is specified by the radiation type and
energy. A servo-controlled motor then moves the selected filter in an arc and into position on the
beam axis.
4.2.2 Description of the collimator system
The collimator is a tungsten and lead assembly that can rotate in one direction only. The
collimator is installed adjacent to the radiation output from the target/window assembly.
The collimator has a beam limiting assembly which has two positions, HOME and LOCKED. The
software selects the two positions, which are specified by the type of irradiation and energy
necessary. The beam limiting assembly has two apertures:
• One open aperture is automatically selected for low energy X-ray mode and for all
electron radiation modes.
• The other aperture is dedicated to the high energy X-ray mode and has a beam-
hardening filter for the highest energy.

Product description
System Description of the Beam Physics System
4.2.3 Description of the secondary filter system
The secondary filter system has a carrier that rotates, installed on the output side of the
collimator. It has five filter positions:
• The flattening filters are installed in one or two of the filter positions for the X-ray
energies.
• The other three filter positions are available for scattering filters for different electron
energies.
The software selects the applicable filter disc, which is specified for the radiation type and energy.
4.2.4 Description of electron applicators and shadow trays
X-ray treatments use shadow trays to put blocks in position in the treatment field. This lets you
safely deliver radiation for fields of irregular shape.
Electron applicators let the user get a regular, clearly defined, treatment field of the necessary
dimensions.
For more information on electron applicators and shadow trays, see the Corrective Maintenance
Manual - Accessories.
4.3 System Description of the Beam Physics System
4.3.1 Operation of the beam focus system
The first part of the linear accelerator waveguide has two sets of focus coils. They give a static,
axial, magnetic field that has a focus effect on the electron beam. The magnetic field is necessary
to adjust for less focus from parts of the RF fields in the waveguide structure of the linear
accelerator.
It is most important to give focus to the electron beam at the input to the linear accelerator
waveguide. This decreases as the electron beam energy increases along the waveguide. The focus
field stops approximately at the center of the waveguide length.
The two sets of focus coil sets are:
• Focus 1 has two water-cooled coils (L1 and L2) at the gun end of the waveguide.
• Focus 2 has one air-cooled coil (L3) and two water-cooled coils (L4 and L5).
The focus coil currents are set to get the maximum output intensity of the radiation beam.
4.3.2 Operation of the power supply to the focus coils
Two thyristor controlled, current stable PSUs energize the focus coils. The PSUs are installed in the
gantry drum (Area 13). Each PSU has a full-wave controlled bridge-rectifier and an LV control PCB
(type A).
The LV control PCBs are as follows:
• Focus 1, PCB13F
• Focus 2, PCB13E.
The LV control PCBs and other LV PSU parts can be replaced in the field. For more information on
the LV PSUs, see the Corrective Maintenance Manual - Power Supplies and Interlocks.

The focus 1 and focus 2 circuits are closed loops and almost the same in operation. Each circuit

• PCB13E for focus 2.

multiplexer sends one value at a time to software item Item 394 through one channel on AI12-

Each LV control PCB senses the voltage across a shunt resistor to monitor the coil current. This
current value is output as a control signal in the range 0 mA to 10 mA. The LV control PCBs send
the control signal through A12-RAB and SCC-RAB to the TCS. The focus system applies servo
control to make sure that the value of the current monitored is at the calibrated value.
The focus coil voltages are monitored and sent back to the TCS to give the interlocks. The servo
systems do not use the voltages, but have set limits that show if a short circuit is present in the
coils.
4.3.4 Operation and analysis of the beam centering system
The electron beam in the linear accelerator waveguide can have a small deviation from the axis of
control. This can be because the digital accelerator waveguide has small symmetrical differences
or the effect of external force (magnetic fields or movements because of gravity).
The waveguide has two sets of beam centering coils that adjust for such effects:
• One set (1R, 1T) is installed at the gun end of the waveguide and aligns the beam to
the control axis
• The other set (2R, 2T) is installed approximately at the middle of the waveguide, after
the center joint. These control the angle of the beam entry into the of the beam
bending system.
Each set of centering coils has two pairs of coils that give two magnetic fields at right angles to
each other and to the electron beam. The centering coil sets align to transmit the radial (R) and
transverse (T) movements. These movements put the electron beam in the center after it comes
through the bending system.
A universal motor drive (UMD) PCB, supplies power to each pair of centering coils. The UMD PCBs
can supply an output of ±600 mA to control the size and direction of the magnetic fields. The
UMD PCBs are installed in the motor drive PCB rack (Area 11).
The centering coils apply the beam corrections, which have to change in cycles as the gantry
angle changes. Although the effects are small, they can cause an effect on the symmetry of the
radiation field with gantry rotation. The control servos of the 2R and 2T centering coils remove
the effects of these changes.
The control servos get feed-forward signals from the gantry angle and use sine/cosine lookup
tables (LUTs). Signals from the detector plates in the ion chamber control the beam around the
LUTs in the prescribed limits.
4.3.4.1 Operation of the 2T servo
For 4 MV the 2T servo is not enabled because of its relation to the point shape on target. The
lower the photon energy is, the larger the spot appears in the A-B Axis. For 4 MV the point shape
is almost the same size as the primary collimator port.
This means that the 2T servo will probably operate incorrectly, therefore the learn function of the
Look-up tables (LUT) and the operation of the servo in auto can be a problem.
The FFF beams do not have the same enhancement for the 2TA and 2TB plate signals as for a
hardened flattening filter. This means that changes in the 2T steering current have only a minor
effect on moving the beam, the field appears to tilt. Again this means that the 2T servo will
probably operate incorrectly.
Electrons operate in almost the same way as FFF beams. The beam hardening is not sufficient and
leads to sensitivity in the A-B Axis. This means that the 2T servo will probably operate incorrectly.

4.3.5 Operation of the beam bending system
The bending magnet assembly (installed in Area 23) has three different electromagnets that
operate together to give an achromatic triple-magnet array. The evacuated flight tube contains
the electron beam and is installed between the poles of the bending magnet assembly (see
Figure 4.4 and Figure 4.5).

The bending magnet assembly includes three sets of electro-magnetic coils, which are connected
in series. Currents of up to 165 amps pass through the electro-magnetic coils. This main current is
called bending coarse. The last magnet in the set has an extra current, called bending fine, which
passes through it. This current is required as the bend in the last magnet is 112° and the bend
angle does not have a linear relationship to the current.
Magnet 1 does an analysis of the energy, and its magnetic field bends the electron beam through
an angle of 44°. The poles of magnet 1 are a special shape which gives distribution of the energy
along the beam path. This results in an energy separation range at the output of magnet 1. The
system adjusts the field strength of magnet 1 to transmit the electrons of the necessary average
energy.

System Description of the Beam Physics System
Magnet 2 removes the 44° bend effect and gives a focus effect in two orthogonal directions. In
this region of the beam path, the widest energy distribution occurs before the start of the beam
convergence.
The converging beam from magnet 2 is directed at magnet 3. The poles of magnet 3 have a
special shape which bends the beam through an angle of 112° to complete the two-dimensional
focus effect. The last beam focus is to an area of approximately 2 mm in diameter at the target.
The six coils of the three magnets, two on each magnet, are water-cooled. Each coil has a thermal
switch connected in series in a temperature circuit which prevents the overheating of the bending
magnet.
The PSUs of the bending magnet have two power supplies (see Figure 4.6) which are part of the
low voltage PSU in Area 13. Thyristors control the two, current-stabilized, power supplies. The
primary supply (PSU1) contains a three-phase, full wave controlled, bridge rectifier and three
PCBs for LV PSU control as follows:
• Phase A: LV PSU type B (PCB13A)
• Phase B: LV PSU type B (PCB13B)
• Phase C: LV PSU type A (PCB13C).

4.3.5.3 Operation of the beam bending control circuit
Two currents bending coarse and bending fine are applied to the channels 6 and 7 of the AO12-A
PCB. This PCB gives two control signals in the range 0 mA to 10 mA. These are applied to the
control input of:
• LV PSU PCB13C for bending coarse
• LV PSU PCB13G for bending fine.
A multiplexer changes the set current values for the two circuits to one channel on the AO12-A
PCB. These values sent to the TCS as Item 394 through the AI12-RHA and SCC-RHA PCBs.
Two LV PSU control PCBs (PCB13C and PCB13G) monitor the current of the bending magnet coil.
These sense the voltage across resistors in the current path of each PSU. The TCS receives these
signals through AI12-RHA and SCC-RGA. The signals apply servo control to the bending coarse
and bending fine set current values. Software item Item 384 monitors the voltage across the coils
through the AI12-RHB and SCC-RHB PCBs.
The six temperature cutouts on the coils are connected in series. During usual conditions, they
hold input A2 on DIE-RHB to ground (digital 0). Operation of a cutout sets the digital channel
condition to 1. Software item Item 094 sets the digital accelerator to the System On condition.

System Description of the Beam Physics System
The target shift system moves the target assembly into one of the two home positions. Home
position 1 is the electron mode, in which the target shift assembly puts a thin nickel window in
the beam path. Home position 2 is the X-ray mode, in which the target shift assembly puts a
tungsten, rhenium and copper target in the beam path.
The drive assembly of the target shift system is installed in Area 24, adjacent to the bending
magnet assembly. A motor and worm gear move the flight tube (and target) horizontally between
the two home positions. A stainless steel bellows lets the flight tube move in relation to the
waveguide, but keeps the vacuum.
Two switches (SW1 and SW3) identify the target position, while switch (SW4) makes sure that the
target is in one of the two home positions. A potentiometer gives a target position indication. See
the table for more information about the code switches.

4.3.6.3 Operation of the control circuit of the target shift system
The PCBs installed in the RHCA (area 12) control the operation of the target shift system.
The software sends a signal to get the position of the target assembly. A PLD on DIE RHB gives an
output signal which operates two relays (RLA1 and RLA3) on the ROC RHB.
The contacts of relays RLA1 and RLA3 are configured to give a bipolar output supply to the target
shift motor. Therefore, when the movement is completed, a short circuit applied to the motor
circuit stops the motor immediately. The target assembly can then move to the set position.
The PLD on DIE RHB receives three control bits:
• Global state is a 2-bit code that shows the global state of the digital accelerator. The
target assembly moves only when this code is 5 or 13 (the Preparatory) or Pause
machine states).
• Target enable is a 1-bit code that prevents operation of the target shift system.
• Target select is a 1-bit code that selects position 1 or position 3 (home position 1 or 2).
4.3.7 Operation of the primary filter assembly
The primary filter is a scatter filter which has six positions. The primary filter attaches to a
movable carrier and operates between the target/electron window and the top of the collimator
(Area 25).
Five of the six filter positions are filled with different thicknesses of tantalum. Each filter is
selected to control the specified level of the electron energy. The sixth position is an open hole
that you can only use during X-ray mode.
The software selects the applicable filter to control the radiation type and energy. A servo motor
moves the selected filter in an arc into position on the beam axis. The servo motor is on the
primary scatter drive unit assembly.
The motor and drive gear (M1), limit microswitches (SW16 and SW17), and position
potentiometer (RV4) are installed on the primary scatter drive unit assembly. All these items are
on the side of the collimator (see Figure 4.7).

4.3.7.3 Operation of the control circuit of the primary filter
The software item Item 321 reads the values, from 1 to 6, which are related to the six carrier
positions. For each of these values, software items Item 531 and Item 532 set applicable values
for the scatter drive and offset positions. The system changes these values to analog voltage and
current levels to give the negative inputs to two amplifiers on DIE RHA. The difference between
the scatter drive and offset values gives a position in which the scatter filter mechanism is stable.
Two potentiometers give feedback on the position of the filter carrier:
• RV4 installed adjacent to the motor on the drive unit
• RV2 installed on the opposite side of the collimator.
The potentiometer RV4 gets a +10V reference voltage from AI12 RHA, which SCC RHA makes
stable. The potentiometer RV2 gets a +10V reference voltage from AI12-RHB, which SCC RHB
makes stable.
The potentiometer RV2 supplies an input to the comparator circuit on DIE RHA. This circuit
compares the position of the scatter filter carrier against preset values in Item 531 and Item 532.
The output of the comparator supplies a logic input to the PLD on DIE RHA. The PLD controls the
scatter filter motor through relays RLA1 and RLA3, which move the scatter carrier motor to the
necessary position.
The contacts of the two relays are configured to give a bipolar output supply to the scatter filter
motor. Therefore when the movement is completed, a short circuit applied to the motor circuit
stops the motor immediately.
The potentiometers RV4 and RV2 send the position of the scatter filter position through the AI12
and SCC PCBs to the TCS. Software items Item 446 and Item 447 read these values.
The scatter filter system uses a code system to monitor the position of the filter. The code also
shows when the mechanism is locked into position.
The scatter filter is enabled for movement when the global state software code is 5 (Preparatory
machine state) and i134 is enabled.
Operation of the hardware limit switches SW16 or SW17 causes an inhibit on the PLD on DIE RHA
and software item Item 517. This stops all movements.
4.3.8 Operation of the collimator system
The collimator is a two position beam limiting assembly that is installed adjacent to the radiation
output from the target/window assembly.
The software item Item 320 controls the two positions of the collimator, where 1 = position 1 and
2 = position 2. The type of radiation selected gives the prescribed collimator position.
The collimator is a tungsten and lead assembly that can rotate in one direction only. The
collimator assembly locks in one of two positions, HOME and LOCKED. An open aperture (no
restriction) is automatically selected for low energy X-ray mode and for all electron radiation
modes. The other aperture is dedicated to the high energy X-ray mode and has a beam hardening
filter.
Motor M1 and the drive gear parts are the same types as those used in the secondary filter
system. The collimator filter system uses a larger gear override subassembly.
A number of microswitches send the collimator status to the TCS:

Product description
System Description of the Beam Physics System
4.3.9 Operation and analysis of the secondary filter system
The secondary filter system has a filter carrier that rotates to five positions. The filter carrier is
installed on the output side of the collimator immediately before the ionization chamber. The
flattening filters are installed in one or two of the filter positions for the X-ray energies. The other
three filter positions are available for scatter filters for different electron energies. Software
selects the applicable filter for the specified radiation type and energy. See the system diagram
4513 330 7042 for more information about the secondary filter system .
The motor M2 and drive gear parts (327 and 328) are the same type as those used in the
collimator system. The secondary filter system has a smaller gear override subassembly.
A number of microswitches monitor the position of the filter carrier and send the information to
the control system:
• SW1 to SW4 installed around the edge of the filter carrier, identify the filter position
with a code system.
• SW5 and SW13 close when the filter carrier locks in position.
• SW9 installed on the gear override subassembly, closes while the filter carrier moves.
You can see the microswitches, other than SW5 and SW13, when you remove the BLD from the
collimator. You must remove the filter carrier to get access to SW5 and SW13.
• Motor
• Motor thermistor TH3
• Protection diode D6
• Drive motor
• Gearbox
• Gearbox filter
• Gear override subassembly, small
• Microswitch with lever SW1 - SW4, SW9
• Microswitch SW5, SW13.

Repairing the system
Tools and test equipment
6.1 Tools and test equipment
WARNING 6.1
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
When you exchange a part you can affect the machine calibration. Record the details of all parts
that are exchanged during fault repair. When a fault has been repaired, perform all test, set up
and calibration procedures that are specified in the respective exchange procedures.
Modifications or work that does not have approval by Elekta, and is not done by an Elekta
approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.
6.2 Removing and installing the flight tube
6.2.1 Important safety information
When you operate or do work on the equipment, Elekta recommends that you use the applicable
radiation protection features, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity for the jurisdiction in which the
equipment is installed.
WARNING 6.2
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. The risk can be excessive exposure to radioactivity. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause serious injury.
WARNING 6.3
Do not do not do maintenance work if the induced radioactivity is more than 50 µSv/hr. For
instruments calibrated in other units, the indication must not be more than the equivalent
value. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
WARNING 6.4
Do not work do maintenance work unless you wear the correct protective equipment when you
touch parts with possible radioactive contamination. This will prevent excessive exposure to
radiation from radioactive tungsten particles in the flight tube and equivalent parts. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Not controlled when downloaded or printed
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702098_09
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 113

Removing and installing the flight tube

6.2.2 Sending an unserviceable flight tube to Elekta
WARNING 6.5
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
1 Measure the level of induced radioactivity at a distance of 1 m from the packaging.
2 Make sure that the level of induced radioactivity is no greater than 5 mSv/h or the
equivalent
3 If all the measured levels of induced radioactivity are below the values specified, it is not
necessary to identify the contents of the packaging as radioactive.
4 If one or more of the measured levels are greater than the values specified, the packaging
must be identified as radioactive.
6.2.3 Removing the lead shielding from the gantry arm
WARNING 6.6
Do not touch the lead weights without protective gloves. Obey the applicable Health and Safety
procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1 Remove the covers from the BLD.
2 Remove the covers from the gantry arm.
3 Rotate the gantry to -90° (or 270°).
4 Remove the two M6 nuts and bolts that attach the tungsten plate to the gantry arm box
(see Figure 6.1).
5 Remove the tungsten plate.

6.2.4 Removing the primary collimator
Prerequisites
Special tools:
• Hoist
• Lifting straps
• Eye bolts.
WARNING 6.7
Record the part numbers of the replacement parts when you replace a part. Do the applicable
test, set up, and calibration procedures for the replacement parts before clinical operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 6.8
Do not do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the electrical supply to the work
area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.9
Do not touch the primary collimator, primary filter and secondary filters, or other parts near to
them until a check for induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is
dangerous. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1 Use the column rotation of the treatment table to rotate the treatment table to 90° or –90°.
2 Lower the bellows cover of the treatment table.
You have sufficient space to use the lifting equipment and complete this procedure.
3 Set the gantry to 0°.
4 Do an X-ray to light field test and record the results.
5 Rotate the gantry to 180°.
6 Rotate the BLD to ±90°.

Removing and installing the flight tube
7 Install the bolts to attach a locking bar at each end of the gantry drum to:
– The center of the support wheel.
– The attachment point on the outer surface of the gantry drum.
8 At the gun end of the gantry arm, install the bolts to attach the rear support bar to:
– The support bracket.
– The nut at the attachment point in the site floor.
9 At the reeling interface cabinet (RIC), set the circuit breaker CB7 to off.
10 Remove the BLD. Refer to the Corrective Maintenance Manual for that BLD, for more
information.
11 Remove sufficient lead shielding from the gantry arm to lift the collimator.
12 Remove the three nuts and washers that attach the collimator to the gantry arm.
13 Disconnect the red cable from the bending fine magnet on the side of the gantry arm.
14 Disconnect the ground (earth) cables from the PCB on the side of the collimator.
15 Disconnect all the connectors from the mounting bracket on the side of the collimator.
16 Install the two eye bolts on the collimator.
17 Attach the lifting straps to the eye bolts and the hoist.
18 Carefully lift the collimator away from the gantry arm.
19 Make sure that the cables do not catch on the equipment while you lift the collimator.
20 Put the collimator on a clean work surface away from the work area.
21 Install the target shield.
6.2.5 Replacing the bearings of the primary collimator
Prerequisites
Special tools
• Hoist
• Lifting straps
• Eye bolts
• Ion chamber protective covers
• Primary collimator lifting jig.
1 Rotate the gantry to 180°.
2 Rotate the BLD to ±0°.
3 Remove the BLD covers.
4 Remove the inner arm covers.
5 Remove the BLD (refer to the applicable BLD Corrective Maintenance Manual).
6 Do the steps that follow to remove the ion chamber.
a Install the top aperture plate to protect the ion chamber.
b Remove the connector D15 and the dose temperature probe from the lead ion
chamber leg.
c Remove the two M5 cap head bolts that hold the ion chamber legs to the mounting
posts and carefully remove the ion chamber.
d Install the bottom aperture plate to protect the ion chamber.
e Move the ion chamber safely away from the work area.
7 Make a mark on the drive and the driven gears of the primary collimator (see Figure 6.4).

WARNING 6.10
Be careful when you tighten the nuts to pull up the primary collimator. The Loctite® bond to the

h Continue to tighten the top nuts in turn until the primary collimator is clear of the

The top collimator bearing will come out. The bottom collimator bearing stays in the
casting.
j Move the primary collimator safely away from the work area.

6 Do the steps that follow to remove the bottom collimator bearing.

6.2.5.2 Installing the bearings of the primary collimator
1 Do the steps that follow to install the new bearings.
a Clean the mating faces of the primary collimator and collimator base casting.
b Apply radiation resistant grease to the balls and races of the two bearings to lubricate
them.
c Clean the inner face of the inner race of a new bearing and apply Loctite® 641™ to its
inner face.
d Install the bearing to the top of the primary collimator.
Note: It can be necessary to tap the bearing lightly into position. To prevent damage, tap only on the
inner race of the bearing.
e Clean the outer face of the new bearing.
f Apply Loctite® 641™ to the outer face of the outer race of the other new bearing.
g Install the bearing to the collimator base casting from below.
Note: It can be necessary to tap the bearing lightly into position. To prevent damage, tap only on the
outer race of the bearing.
h Let a 60 minute interval occur to let the Loctite® cure.
2 Do the steps that follow to install the primary collimator into the base casting.
a Install the lifting jig to the primary collimator.
b Attach a lifting strap to the eyebolt and lift the primary collimator with the hoist to
the work area.
c Lower the primary collimator into the aperture in the base casting.

6.2.6 Removing the flight tube
Prerequisites
Half length wrench (10 mm AF)
Container 10 L.
When you replace a flight tube, put the unserviceable flight tube in the packaging of the
replacement flight tube. This will minimize the possible excessive exposure to radioactivity.
WARNING 6.11
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
WARNING 6.12
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
1 Remove the BLD.
2 Remove the collimator.
3 Set the vacuum circuit breaker CB10, and water pump circuit breaker CB11 to off.
4 Pressurize the vacuum system to room pressure with dry nitrogen (refer to the Corrective
Maintenance Manual - Cooling, Gas and Vacuum Systems.
5 Put the two inlet valves at the reeling post or the RIC to the open position to release the
internal water pressure.
6 Close the valves when the internal pressure decreases to 0.
7 Prevent leakage from the cooling water system when you disconnect the hoses at the tube
and water transfer blocks as follows:
a Fold the cooling water hoses to the flight tube and magnet coils.
b Install cable ties as necessary to hold the cooling water hoses away from the work
area.
c Put the container in position below the connections of the cooling water hoses in the
gantry arm.
d Disconnect the cooling water hoses.
e Put the ends of the cooling water hoses in the container and let the water drain into
the container.
8 At the manifolds on each side of the gantry arm, disconnect the cooling water hoses of the
bending magnet and the flight tube.
9 Put a cap on the hose connections of the bending magnet and the flight tube to prevent the
leakage of water.

6.2.8 Installing the collimator
Prerequisites
Special tools
• Hoist
• Lifting straps
• Eye bolts.
WARNING 6.16
Record the part numbers of the replacement parts when you replace a part. Do the applicable
test, set up, and calibration procedures for the replacement parts before clinical operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 6.17
Do not do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the electrical supply to the work
area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
1 Make sure that the dowel pins, which hold the collimator to the magnet plate, are correctly
in their positions.
2 Attach the lifting straps to the eye bolts.
3 Carefully lift and move the collimator in position for installation.
4 Before you lower the collimator, make sure that all the cables are clear of the installation
area.
WARNING 6.18
Do not put your hands below the collimator as it is lowered. If it is necessary, use wood blocks
to hold the weight of the collimator. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury and
damage to equipment.
Note: If it is necessary to lift the collimator during the installation, you must lift it clear of the dowel pins.
This lets you make sure that the location of the dowel pins in the magnet plate is correct. You can
then engage the collimator on the dowel pins, fully lower the collimator, and attach it to the
magnet plate.
5 Slowly and carefully lower the collimator. Make sure that the cables to the bending fine
magnet does not catch in the equipment.

6.3 Removing and installing the bending magnet assembly
6.3.1 Special tools and materials for the bending magnet assembly
The special tools and materials that follow are necessary to remove and install the bending
magnet assembly:
• Plastic bag
• Dowel extractor tool
• Light machine oil or equivalent (for example, WD40)
• Abrasive pads
• Pink wire with heat resistant braid sleeve
• Soft face hammer
• Loctite® 222 or equivalent
• Insulated straps
• Flight tube seal
• Cooling water hoses.
6.3.2 Removing the bending magnet assembly
WARNING 6.19
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.

WARNING 6.20
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
WARNING 6.21
Do not do work on the bending magnet until you install the pit prop between the gantry arm
and the floor. The pit prop holds the gantry arm in position. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause serious injury.
WARNING 6.22
Do not touch the lead weights without protective gloves. Obey the applicable Health and Safety
procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1 Remove the flight tube.

6.3.3 Installing the bending magnet assembly
1 Clean the magnet blocks with an abrasive pad and a light oil, for example, WD40.
2 Install the clamps, screws and washers to attach the new coils to the magnet blocks.
3 If it is necessary, lightly tap the clamps with a small, soft face hammer to make the clamps
tight.
4 Apply a small quantity of Loctite® 222.
5 Install the bolts to attach the magnet blocks to the side plates.
6 Install the C shape tungsten piece around coil 1.
7 Use the pink wire kept during the removal as a template to cut lengths of pink wire to the
correct length.
8 Install the pink wires through the clamps and connect to the temperature switches in series.
9 Put a small quantity of oil on the mating faces of the magnet and assemble the magnet.
CAUTION 6.3
Correctly align the magnet before you install the dowel bolts. The dowel bolts can easily break
if the magnet is not correctly aligned and you use too much force. Install the four dowel bolts
with your hand only, until they are fully hand tight. You can then tighten the bolts to a torque of
16 Nm.
10 Put a small quantity of oil on the screw threads of the four dowel bolts. Do not use Loctite®
adhesive on these bolts.
11 Install the tungsten bridge piece on the bottom. Apply Loctite® 222 to the screws.
12 Install the lead shielding to the front of the assembly.
13 Install the target shift mechanism at the mark identified during removal.
14 Install the insulated straps at the marks identified during removal to connect the two halves
of the magnet together.
15 Make the remaining connections to the temperature switch.
16 Install the top plate of the magnet.
17 If you install a new flight tube, install and adjust the top and lower shims.
18 Attach the lifting frame to the bending magnet assembly.
19 Attach the lifting straps/sling to the bending magnet assembly and the hoist.
20 Lift the bending magnet assembly and move it into position for installation.
21 Carefully lower the bending magnet assembly into the box section of the gantry arm. Make
sure that you lower the bending magnet assembly around the drift tube (bellows).
22 Install the dowels in the magnet plate in the positions identified during removal.
23 Set the multimeter to the Ohms range. Make sure that there is a high resistance (>1 MW) to
ground from the magnet connections.
24 Make sure that there are no short circuits, specially where the straps go below the
assembly.
25 Install the flight tube.
Note: If you install a new flight tube, you must install a new flight tube seal.
26 Install the cooling water hoses, but do not tighten the hose connections too much.
27 Pressurize the water system.
a Give the location, set the water pump circuit breaker CB11 to on.
b Bleed the water system to remove the air (refer to the Corrective Maintenance
Manual - Cooling, Gas and Vacuum Systems.
c Visually examine the water pipes and hose connections for leaks.

7.1 Doing the test and set up of the beam physics movements
7.1.1 Doing a test of the primary filter operation
This test moves the primary filter to different positions, you can then do a check to make sure
that it operates correctly.
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Click Display Service Pages.
3 Load the Mechanisms service page.
4 Click Deliver Quick Beam.
5 Make sure that the machine state is Preparatory and that movements are enabled.
6 Select XRAY.
7 Select the required x-ray energy.
8 Click Load.
9 Record the values of item 446 Scatt.posn and item 447 Scatt.chk from the Mechanisms
service page.
10 In Deliver Quick Beam, select Electron.
11 Select an electron energy.
12 Click Load.
13 Record the values of item 446 Scatt.posn and item 447 Scatt.chk from the Mechanisms
service page.
14 Repeat relevant steps for all other electron energies.
15 In the Mechanisms service page, select Item 321 Sctr. ctrl.
16 In Item 321 Sctr. ctrl, type different primary filter values in the range 0 to 4000.
17 For each of the values do the checks that follow.
a Make sure that the primary filter moves.
b Make sure that there is a change to the Actual value of Item 446 Scatt posn.
c Make sure that there is a change to the Actual value of Item 447 Scatt chk.
18 Make sure that movement of the primary filter stops when the limit switch (SW16 or
SW17), that is applicable to the direction of movement, operates.
Note: To view the primary scatter filter positions it is necessary to remove the BLD, select and load an
electron energy and remove the electron secondary filter, which is over the primary collimator.
Pressing the touchguard override button will enable movements to allow the filters to move.
19 Adjust the primary filter until you can see one edge of the hole through the hole of the
primary collimator.
20 Record the values of Item 446 Scatt posn and Item 447 Scatt chk.
Note: The primary scatter value that is necessary to align the foil position with the collimator is usually
the center position. The data from the position and check potentiometers do not have to agree
with the necessary setting. But, the data from the position potentiometer and the check
potentiometer must agree in limits.
21 Do steps 19 and 20 again for the opposite edge of the hole.
The center value between the indications is the center position of the hole.
22 Repeat for the five foil positions.

7.1.3 Doing a test of the secondary filter operation
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Click Display Service Pages.
3 Load the Mechanisms service page.
4 In the Mechanisms service page, select Item 322 Fltr ctrl .
5 In the Set box, type 1.
This tells the secondary filter to move to position 1.
6 Make sure that the secondary filter moves.
7 Make sure that the Actual value of Item 203 Filter mon shows the value 6.
8 Click Override Machine Items.
The Override Machine Items dialog box appears.
9 While the secondary filter is in movement, make sure that the messages that follow appear
in the list of inhibits:
– FILTER MOVING 535
– FILTER LOCKED 536
10 Make sure that the messages clear when the secondary filter moves to its locked position.
Note: A drive pin, which is installed in a slot in the drive gear wheel, operates the filter mechanism.
CAUTION 7.2
Make sure that there is not too much movement of the pin in the slot as this can cause damage
to the microswitch. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
11 Make sure that the filter mechanism locks.
12 After the mechanism locks make sure that:
– The pin is approximately in the center of the slot

7.3.3.1 Doing a test of the gun servo stability
This procedure does a test to find if the gun servo is stable at nominal dose rates of 25 MU and
400 MU. The test examines the number of oscillations at the correct (100%) value of the gun
current.

6 Click Override Machine Item.

8 Click Load.

7.6 Description of set up and check of the rotational beam
physics
The 2R/2T uses a look up table, a set of calibration blocks and the ion chamber plates to get an
analysis of its output.
Only the calibration block value, that is an offset applied to each position, is given by the energy.
Before radiation, the output is set to the look up table value. This is an index of the gantry angle
which is mixed with the set or calibration block value. This output changes when it receives valid
signals from the ion chamber. The output cannot be different from the initial default by more
than the maximum and minimum limits in the servo.
As the gantry angle changes, the look up tables adjust for changes as is necessary (see Figure 7.2
for a graph of the 2R/2T servo).

7.7 Description of checking and setting the wedge look-up
tables
As this is not a correct tilt, the introduction of a mirror image waveform cancels the error signal.
This is the same size and in the opposite phase. The wedge look up table holds this mirror image
waveform. The waveform and is then put together with the 2R and 2T error signals when the
wedge is set into the field.

Setting up, testing and calibrating the system
Description of checking and setting the wedge look-up tables
Item 127, 2R Err or Item 128, 2T Err Part 136 changes the size of the look up table sine wave. Part
137 of the same two items changes the phase of the look up table sine wave. It can then be set in
the opposite phase to the error table, which removes the error.
Therefore, with the servos in the manual position, Item 127, 2R Err and Item 128, 2T Err show a
value of less than ±1.0 when the X-ray BLD is rotated.
7.7.1 Configuring the wedge
This procedure applies to all wedge types.
Before you start the wedge look up, make sure that the set-up of the A-B direction and G-T
direction symmetry is accurate. Also adjust the 2R and 2T balance to give a zero error for A-B
direction and G-T direction symmetry (2R and 2T in Manual).
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Rotate the BLD to -180°.

Note: A typical value is 50 to 500 for the -180° position.
5 Rotate the BLD to 0° and note the value of Item 136 D.rot. Chk, Part 111
6 Use the formula that follows to calculate the index gain.
16 x 4096
Index Gain =
Part 111 (0°) - Part 111 (-180°)
Note: A typical result is between 35 and 39.
7 Type the index gain value in Item 127 2R Err, Part 134 and Item 128 2T Err, Part 134.
8 Click Save.

7.8 Doing a functional check of the X-ray start and stability
7.8.1 Test sequence for the X-ray start and stability
1 Do a linear accelerator irradiation test.
2 Let the temperature of the linear accelerator decrease for 10 minutes with the HT off.
3 Do the short term stability test. This is referred to as the cold start test.
4 Let the temperature of the linear accelerator decrease for 1 minute with the HT off.
5 Do the short term stability test again. This is referred to as the hot start test.
6 Let the temperature of the linear accelerator decrease for 5 minutes with the HT off.
7 Do the long term stability test.
8 Do steps 1 to 7 again for all available X-ray energies.
7.8.2 Doing a test of the short term stability
Prerequisites
This test stimulates the clinical beams used during treatment at the maximum dose rate of the
linear accelerator at all X-ray energy levels. Start the tests with the highest X-ray energy.
1 Click the Service Graphing button.

7.8.4 Examining the stability test results
1 If reverse diode overloads (magnetron arcing) occurred during the test, do the test again.
2 If the magnetron arcing continues to occur during the test, condition the magnetron.
3 If the magnetron does not condition after you run the HT, reject the magnetron because of
too much arcing. Do not reject the magnetron because the dose rate is not stable.
4 If you reject a magnetron because the dose rate is not stable, you must record the
information that follows:
– The test failures (for example, hot start, cold start, short term, or long term)
– The X-ray energies that were successful and not successful
– The linear accelerator serial number
– The site of the test
– The name of the Operator
– The related history.

7.10 Calibrating the nominal dose rate
The calibrated nominal dose rate gives the possible dose rate for the delivery of a segment at a
given energy. Each energy has a calibrated nominal dose rate. Part 134 of Item 44 holds this value
in the Energy Cal. Block.
This procedure is only applicable if the actual dose rate is frequently lower than or higher than
the necessary dose rate.
Note: Make sure that the linear accelerator is warmed up before you start the calibration of the nominal
dose rate.

Note: For linear accelerators with FTMs, the limit of the stability timer is 2.5 seconds. If the actual dose
rate is less than 75% of the specified dose rate, it can cause the beam to terminate. This can occur
during a slow start where the low dose rate monitor records before you increase to the specified
dose rate.

The green line Item 665 Part 4 shows the dose rate error in 10ths %. The necessary
condition is that the dose rate becomes stable at a value other than zero, before the end of
the beam. Three important points are that the:
– Interlock is active and measures the dose rate error in 10ths %. Item 665 Part 4 shows
this value.
– Dose rate error becomes stable in the default ±25% limit.
– Item 665 Part 156 value, which is in 1000ths of an MU because it collects values that
are fractions, stays at 0. The yellow line, Item 665 Part 131, shows the limit.

Test equipment

13 Click Load.

17 Change the ionization curve to a dose curve.

centimeters.
19 Use the formula that follows to calculate the energy E.
E = 3 × R80
20 For the energy to be correct, the values from the formula must agree with the energy that
is necessary.

value. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.

to radiation from radioactive tungsten particles in the flight tube and equivalent parts. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
